coredata.dc:title: Independent hydrothermal synthesis of the undoped, nitrogen, boron and sulphur doped biogenic carbon nanodots and their potential application in the catalytic chemo-reduction of Alizarine yellow R azo dye
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Dhanush, C.
Sethuraman, M.G.
coredata.dc:description: This research study highlights the catalytic usage of hetero atoms doped and undoped biogenic carbon nano dots (BCNDs) in the reduction of Alizarine yellow R (AYR) dye. Hydrothermal route was followed to synthesize the eco-friendly and fluorescent undoped as well as, N, B & S doped BCNDs from Syzygium cumini (S. cumini) fruit extract. Synthesized BCNDs exhibited good fluorescent and optical properties. From the HR-TEM results, the sizes of the spherically shaped undoped, N, B & S doped BCNDs were found to be 4.75 nm, 4.31 nm, 4.07 nm & 3.96 nm respectively. XRD results highlighted their amorphous nature. Functional groups and elemental percentages were elucidated from the results of FT-IR, EDS and XPS. Graphitic texture of the BCNDs were explained from Raman spectroscopy results and SAED. Thermal stability of BCNDs was evident from the results of TGA analysis. Further, BCNDs were used as green catalyst in the reduction of Alizarine Yellow R (AYR) dye. Langmuir- Hinshelwood mechanism was applied to evaluate the catalytic influence of BCNDs on AYR dye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Syzygium cumini fruit extract
Biogenic carbon nano dots (BCNDs)
Hydrothermal carbonization
Catalytic potency
Alizarine yellow R (AYR) dye

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assessment of the catalytic potency of BCNDs
Catalytic potency of the BCNDs (all dopants) in the reduction of Alizarine yellow R (AYR) dye:

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Department of Science and Technology- Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial help laid over a research project entitled “Eco-friendly……… Catalysis” (File No: EMR/2016/006024).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Visible absorption spectra of S. cumini fruit extract, undoped & various doped BCNDs.
Fluorescence spectra (emission) of the N-doped BCNDs at various excitations (a) 270–320 nm and (b) 320–400 nm.
(a) HR-TEM image. (b) Size distribution graph. (&) (c) SAED pattern of N-doped BCNDs.
XRD patterns of undoped & various doped BCNDs from S. cumini.
FT-IR spectrum of the S. cumini fruit extract, undoped & various doped BCNDs.
TGA curve of undoped & doped BCNDs.
XPS spectrum of N doped BCNDs, (a) Full survey scan spectrum & deconvoluted high resolution spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s.
Reduction absorption spectra of AYR dye with NaBH4 alone.
Reduction absorption spectra of AYR dye with NaBH4 in the existence of (a) Undoped, (b) N-doped, (c) B-doped & (d) S- doped BCNDs.
Kinetics plot of AYR dye reduction in the existence of (a) Undoped, (b) N-doped, (c) B-doped & (d) S- doped BCNDs.
Atomic percentages of elements present in the BCNDs.
Slope, Intercept (k) and co-efficient (R2) values of the undoped and various doped BCNDs kinetics plot.

head.ce:title: Independent hydrothermal synthesis of the undoped, nitrogen, boron and sulphur doped biogenic carbon nanodots and their potential application in the catalytic chemo-reduction of Alizarine yellow R azo dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University, Gandhigram 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University, Gandhigram 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research study highlights the catalytic usage of hetero atoms doped and undoped biogenic carbon nano dots (BCNDs) in the reduction of Alizarine yellow R (AYR) dye. Hydrothermal route was followed to synthesize the eco-friendly and fluorescent undoped as well as, N, B & S doped BCNDs from Syzygium cumini (S. cumini) fruit extract. Synthesized BCNDs exhibited good fluorescent and optical properties. From the HR-TEM results, the sizes of the spherically shaped undoped, N, B & S doped BCNDs were found to be 4.75 nm, 4.31 nm, 4.07 nm & 3.96 nm respectively. XRD results highlighted their amorphous nature. Functional groups and elemental percentages were elucidated from the results of FT-IR, EDS and XPS. Graphitic texture of the BCNDs were explained from Raman spectroscopy results and SAED. Thermal stability of BCNDs was evident from the results of TGA analysis. Further, BCNDs were used as green catalyst in the reduction of Alizarine Yellow R (AYR) dye. Langmuir- Hinshelwood mechanism was applied to evaluate the catalytic influence of BCNDs on AYR dye.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) fall into a sub division of nanomaterials. CDs were accidentally discovered by Xu et al., in 2004 during the purification process of single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) from the arc- discharge soot [1]. Valcárcel et al., performed a systematic and rational allotment of CDs based on their origin nature, crystalline arrangement and quantum confinement. Based on their classification, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) were reported. Amorphous semi-spheroidal carbon nanodots with defective quantum confinement are treated as carbon nanodots (CNDs). Spheroidal quantum dots with quantum confinement and crystalline arrangements of carbon were classified as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the π-conjugated individual sheets were named as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). These classifications were made based on the precursors used and the method of synthesis adopted for their fabrication [2].

CDs are zero dimensional and their sizes range from 1 to 10 nm. CDs seem to be the latent alternatives for harmful metal based quantum dots (MQDs) due to their alluring features like simple, efficient and inexpensive methods of preparation, long thermal and optical photostability, excitation dependent emission, facile surface functionalization and excellent biocompatibility [3]. The unique and fascinating properties of CDs are low toxicity, high water dispersibility, photostability, chemical inactiveness, fluorescence emission behavior, catalytic activity and excitation dependent emission [4]. Typically, top-down and bottom-up approaches are the two potential approaches for the preparation of CDs. Microwave synthesis, solvothermal reaction, high thermal decomposition and hydrothermal carbonization are categorized as bottom-up approach methods [5–7] Laser-ablation, arc discharge, ultrasonic exfoliation, acid oxidation and electrochemical method are categorized as top-down approach methods [8–10].

The fluorescence emission behavior intensively depends upon size, nature of the dopant and passivating agent, pH of the material and the solvent used [11]. Due to their high fluorescent and catalytic nature, they are used in the fields of

bio-imaging, catalytic and electrocatalytic applications, optoelectronics, drug delivery and sensors [12]. Intrinsic properties, electron distribution and surface structure of the CDs can be fine-tuned by hetero atom doping. Among the various hetero atom dopants, nitrogen seems to be the most efficient dopant because it enhances the quantum yield, electronic strategies and high fluorescence yield of the CDs [13]. Nitrogen provides excess electrons to the precursors because it acts as a source of n-type impurities which alters the optical properties and raises the fermi level [14]. In case of sulphur doped CDs, doping of sulphur optimizes the electronic properties of the CDs. Sulphur provides density of states or it creates the emissive traps for the excited electrons and also it is the reason for the modification of the band gap energy [15]. Upon boron doping, the intrinsic nature of the CDs changes. It is reported that introduction of less electronegative boron atom shelters the charge density of the C and N bonding pattern, which subsequently alters the bonding patterns and results in the decrease of the surface states [16].

Synthetic and green sources have been used for the synthesis of CDs. Researchers focus on the natural sources for the synthesis of CDs, because they are cost-effective, eco-friendly and readily available [17]. In this work S. cumini (Jamun fruit) was utilized as the carbon precursor. S. cumini belongs to Myrtaceae family and it is a evergreen tropical tree [18]. It is native to India and East Indies. They are also found in Myanmar, Afghanistan, Thailand, Philippines and Madagascar [19]. The phytoconstituents present in S. cumini include different types of acids, tannins and flavonoids. They are reported to possess various diverse pharmacological activities like gastroprotective, anti-infective, anti-malarial, anti-bacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and antiulcerogenic activities [20].

It is well known that highly toxic and chemically stable dye pollutants cause adverse health effects on both human and animal life systems. Among the dyes used widely, Alizarine yellow R (AYR) receives utmost attention due to its dangerous ill effects. It belongs to the class of azo dyes and it is a salicylic acid derivative. AYR is mostly used as a pH indicator, as a biological stain in chemical examinations and also in dyeing industries. Due to its complex, aromatic and recalcitrant nature it is non-biodegradable and persists in nature for a long time causing heart diseases, lung cancer, neurotoxicity, skin and respiratory disorders in humans [21]. In this study the catalytic chemo-reduction method using BCNDs was followed to arrest its harmful activity.

This paper reports a comparative study of a simple, facile, hydrothermally synthesized undoped as well as N, B & S doped BCNDs from the S. cumini fruit extract (carbon precursor). BCNDs were doped with N, B and S using

aqueous NH3, boric acid and sulphuric acid as the corresponding reagents for doping. These synthesized BCNDs were subjected to optical, surface morphological and structural characterizations. A comparative application study was also carried out using the undoped and doped BCNDs as green catalysts in the reduction of AYR dye.

Fresh S. cumini fruits were obtained from an organic farm at Dhimbatty village, Kotagiri. Aqueous NH3, sulphuric acid and boric acid were shopped from CDH chemicals. NaBH4 and AYR dye were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Double distilled H2O was used throughout the research work.

About 10 g of fruits were macerated with 50 ml of milli Q-water. Using a mixer grinder, fruits were squashed and then filtered to get a clear fruit extract. The clear fruit extract was utilized as the carbon source for the synthesis of BCNDs.

BCNDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization method. In this method, to the carbon precursor (55 ml extract) and 5 ml reagent (dopants) (i.e. Aqueous ammonia (1 M) for N, boric acid (1 M) for B and sulphuric acid (1 M) for S was added individually to the precursor and synthesized separately in a 100 ml teflon lined autoclave. For the undoped BCNDs only, the carbon precursor was taken as the starting material. A reaction condition of 180 °C was maintained for 12 h in a hot air oven. Using Whatmann-40 filter paper, the resultant dense dark brown shaded solution was refined. Later, BCNDs were collected in a SMF and kept in a cooler at 4 °C for further characterization purposes.

UV–Visible absorbance and fluorescence behavior were analyzed using JASCO V-750 spectrophotometer and JASCO SP 8500 spectrofluorometer respectively. PANalytical X’pert diffractometer was used to record the XRD patterns. HR-TEM instrument (FEI-TECNAI T20 G2) was used to determine the size of the BCNDs. BRUKER Nano, GmBh, D-12489 (Germany)(0-30KeV acc. volt) was used to obtain the EDS data. Jasco-FT-I460 plus was utilized to determine the functional groups present in BCNDs by KBr pellet technique. Laser Raman spectrometer (STR 500 nm focal length) was used to record Raman spectra. TGA instrument (NETZSCH STA-2500 Regulus) was used to study the thermal behavior of the prepared BCNDs. XPS instrument (PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, FEI Inc) was utilized to determine the percentage of respective atoms and groups present in BCNDs at 30 eV scan rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the catalytic potency of BCNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Reduction of AYR dye with the help of NaBH4 was carried out in the presence of undoped as well as N, B & S doped BCNDs separately to assess their catalytic ability. Number of trials were run to optimize the concentrations of both the NaBH4 and AYR dye. To a 3.5 ml glass cuvette (1 cm path length distance), 1.5 ml of AYR dye (0.1 M), 0.5 ml of NaBH4 (0.01 M) (freshly prepared) and 1 ml of water were added and homogenized well. To this reaction mixture, 10 µl of undoped, N-doped, B-doped and S-doped BCNDs were added separately in different cuvettes. The progress of the reduction was observed spectroscopically by checking the absorbance at 375 nm at regular intermissions.

Hydrothermally synthesized undoped and various hetero atoms doped carbon dots were characterized duly by various methods and studied for their efficacy in the reduction of AYR dye. The results of the study are given herewith under.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–Vis spectrum of S. cumini fruit extract and BCNDs. In UV–Vis spectroscopy, the non-bonding and bonding electrons of the molecules absorb the visible or ultraviolet light to excite the electrons to upper anti-bonding orbitals. The fruit extract of S. cumini displayed a shoulder at 274 nm. Undoped BCNDs showed two absorption maxima at 284 and 342 nm. Likewise, N doped BCNDs showed absorption maxima at 272 and 306 nm, B doped BCNDs exhibited absorption maxima at 266 and 338 nm whereas S doped BCNDs showed absorption maxima at 292 and 348 nm. The doping of hetero atoms produces corresponding shifts in the absorption maxima which could be mainly attributed to the π- π* transition of the C=C group and n- π* transition of the C=O/carbon bonded groups of BCNDs [25]. The variation in the electronic environment due to doping causes the change in the energy for the π-π* electronic transitions of C=C and n-π* of carbonyl groups of the phytoconstituents and the dopants which reside on the surface of BCNDs [13]. The absorption strongly depends upon the particle size, medium and chemical surroundings. It is also found that with the decrease in size the absorption spectra becomes broad and less intense. Thus, it may be attributed to the size effect and increase in the availability of the low lying states [26].

(S-doped) (Fig. S.11) in the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, 320 nm, 350 nm and 360 nm respectively. There was a slight increase in the fluorescence intensity and no specific change in the wavelength when the excitation wavelength was raised from 260 to 350 nm and this was due to the π*- π transitions of the graphitic cores of carbon. Accordingly, when the excitation wavelength was raised from 350 to 450 nm, there was a red shift in the emission wavelength and it was owing to the radiative reassociation of π*-n transitions of the non-bonding electrons of nitrogen, boron, thiol and/or carbonyl functional moieties. The excitation dependent emission behavior of the BCNDs were due to the existence of various groups like –OH, –COOH, –NH2, -BH and -SH on the surface of the BCNDs and also variation in the sizes of the BCNDs [27,28]. In the case of S- doped BCNDs, dual emission phenomenon was noted when the excitation wavelength was raised from 400 to 500 nm. This phenomenon was due to the change in the electronic structure and also due to the presence of more surface states on the synthesized S doped BCNDs [29].

Fluorescence excitation spectrum of undoped and doped BCNDs (Fig. S.12 a-d) showed two distinct excitation peaks at 280 nm and 350 nm for undoped BCNDs, 231 nm and 318 nm for N-doped BCNDs, 290 nm and 350 nm for B-doped BCNDs and 285 nm and 355 nm for S-doped BCNDs. The results of the excitation spectra clearly denoted two types of excitation energies trapped by the surface of the synthesized undoped and as well as heteroatom doped BCNDs [30]. This type of excitation-dependent emission behavior of the synthesized undoped and doped BCNDs arises because of their varying sizes and also due to the presence of various groups like –COOH, –OH, –NH2, -B-N, and -SH on the BCNDs surface [28].

HR-TEM analysis is a technique which is widely used to reveal the size, morphology and graphitic nature of the undoped and doped BCNDs. The HR-TEM image of N doped BCNDs is shown in Fig. 3
(a) and for undoped, B and S doped BCNDs are shown in S.1 (a), S.2 (a), S.3 (a) respectively. HR-TEM images were spherical in shape and they were evenly distributed on the copper grid without agglomeration. The sizes of the undoped, N, B and S doped BCNDs were 4.75 nm, 4.31 nm, 4.07 nm and 3.96 nm respectively and their distribution caliber extends from 1.0 nm to 7.0 nm. The diffused SAED patterns of the undoped and doped BCNDs signify the presence of graphitic carbon (sp2) with amorphous nature [31].

EDS spectrum of undoped and doped BCNDs are presented in Fig. S.4. (a)-(d). The undoped BCNDs contained C (76.83%) & O (23.17%). N doped BCNDs had C (61.45%), O (19.76%) & N (18.79%). In B doped BCNDs, C (37.93%), O (44.18%) & B (17.89%) were present. S doped BCNDs consisted C (49.95%), O (38.13%) & S (11.92%). Thus, the results showed the percentage of carbon, oxygen and the corresponding hetero atoms doped on their respective BCNDs.

XRD was employed to explore the phase, amorphous nature and the structure of the BCNDs [32]. The XRD patterns of BCNDs are shown in Fig. 4
. The 2θ values corresponding to the broad peak at 28.470 and weak peak at 40.60 to (002) and (100) lattice facet discretely. In BCNDs, the broad diffraction apex witness that they are amorphous in nature with firmly arranged carbon atoms and it is also due to the functionalization of N, B and S atoms doped on the surface of BCNDs [31,33–35]. The d-spacing values of the broad and weak peaks are 0.312 nm and 0.221 nm respectively. Thus, the above XRD results vouched for the graphitic structure of BCNDs.

Fig. 5
(a-e). display the functional groups of the S. cumini fruit extract and BCNDs through the FT-IR spectrum. FT-IR spectrum of the fruit extract (5a) showed major absorption bands at 3418 cm−1, 2922 cm-1and 1742 cm−1 of –OH, –CH and -C = O groups correspondingly. There were common absorption bands at 3418 cm−1, 2922 cm−1,1742 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 which correspond to the existence of –OH/NH, –CH, -C = O and -C = C- groups [ Fig. 5 (b-e)]. In the FT-IR spectrum, the band around 3000–3500 cm−1 was broadened which could be due to the overlap of –OH/–NH functional stretching bands or by the doping of the hetero atoms on the BCNDs [36]. In nitrogen doped BCNDs (5c), band at 1382 cm−1 revealed the presence of -C-N group [37]. In boron doped BCNDs (5d), there was a band at 1119 cm−1 which showed the presence of -B-C- group [38]. In sulphur doped BCNDs (5e), there were two stretching frequencies at 795 cm−1 and 2537 cm−1 which showed the existence of -C-S and -S-H groups [39,40]. Thus, FT-IR spectrum exposed the functional groups of the fruit extract and as well as the hetero atoms doped on the surface of BCNDs.

Carbon based nanomaterials are characterized well using Raman spectroscopic technique [12]. Raman spectra of undoped and doped BCNDs (Fig. S.5 a-d) showed two distinctive peaks around 1360 cm−1 to 1385 cm−1 (D band) and 1595 cm−1 to 1610 cm−1 (G band). The D band can be ascribed to the distorted sp2 carbon structures and the G band can be accounted for the variations in the ordered sp2 graphitic carbons respectively. The intensities of D and G bands are represented as ID/IG. For an ideal graphitic nature, the ID/IG ratio must be less than 1. The ID/IG ratio of the undoped, N, B, & S doped BCNDs were 0.69, 0.72, 0.58 and 0.61 respectively. Thus these values showed that BCNDs possess lesser defective sites with partial disordered crystal structure [30].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was deployed to assess the thermal stability of BCNDs. TGA curve of undoped and doped BCNDs are shown in Fig. 6
. Up to 600 °C, the weight loss was observed continuously. In case of all the doped BCNDs, at the early stage, there was 10–20% weight loss and this loss was associated to the desorption of the physically adsorbed H2O molecules on the BCNDs. As shown in the spectra, around 250 °C-400 °C, subsequent weight loss could be noticed due to the disintegration of O2 containing moieties. Further when the temperature was raised up to 600 °C, there was about 50% of fall in weight and this was due to the disintegration of functional moieties and the carbon framework of BCNDs [41].

XPS technique was used to investigate the electronic states, elemental mass and details of the BCNDs. Fig. 7 displayed the XPS spectra of the N doped BCNDs and Fig (S.6-S.8) displayed the XPS spectra of undoped, B and S doped BCNDs. The full survey scan spectrum of both undoped and doped BCNDs displayed two definite peaks at 282.4 and 529.6 eV which resemble the presence of elemental carbon and oxygen. In addition, there were additional peaks in the doped BCNDs at 396.8, 225.6 and 151.2 eV which indicated the presence of the elemental nitrogen, boron and sulphur in the respective XPS spectrum. Deconvoluted spectra of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), oxygen (O1s), boron (B1s) and sulphur (S2p) were also interpreted. The deconvoluted C1s of undoped BCNDs (Fig. S.6b) showed three distinct peaks at 284.4, 286.15 and 292.7 eV which correspond to the habitation of C-H, C = C (sp2)/–COO and π-electron delocalization respectively. Deconvoluted O1s (Fig. S.6c) spectrum showed three peaks at 530.3, 531.46 and 532.54 eV of C-OH, -C = O and C-O-C moieties respectively [31,42]. In the case of nitrogen doped BCNDs, deconvoluted C1s (Fig. 7 b) showed peaks at 284.5, 285.7 & 292.5 eV due to the presence of -C = C- (sp2)/ C-H, -C = O/-C = N and π-electron delocalization respectively. Deconvoluted N1s (Fig. 7 c) peaks at 397.9, 399.3 and 400.2 eV correspond to C-NH2/C-N-C, O = C-NH2/-N-H and pyridinic nitrogen groups present in BCNDs. Deconvoluted O1s (Fig. 7 d) peaks at 530.5, 531.6 & 532.9 eV showed the presence C-OH, -C-N-O/-C = O & C-O-C groups [30,43,44].

In boron doped BCNDs, deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. S.7b) displayed three different peaks at 284.4, 285.8 and 288.6 eV which showed the existence of C-B, C = C (sp2) & -C-N. Deconvoluted B1s (Fig. S.7c) spectrum exhibited three different peaks at 190.1, 191 & 192.21 eV which showed the presence of B-C, B-CO2 & B-O groups respectively [33] Deconvoluted O1s (Fig. S.7d) spectrum exhibited three variant peaks at 530.5, 531.6 and 532.4 eV revealing the presence of C-OH, -C = O, and C-O-C groups respectively [44].

In sulphur doped BCNDs, deconvoluted C1s (Fig. S.8b) showed four various peaks at 284.9, 285.5, 287.4 and 289.5 eV which correspond to the existence of C = C (sp2), C-N, -C = O and π- π* electron delocalization. Deconvoluted S2p (Fig. S.8c) showed three binding energy peaks at 162.3, 164.3 and 167.5 eV indicating the existence of S-H/S-C (S2p3/2), C-SO/ -SO3, and S2p1/2 groups, respectively. Deconvoluted O1s spectrum (Fig. S.8d) displayed three different peaks at 529.1, 530.5, 531.8 eV which emphasized the presence of C-OH, -C=O and C-O-C groups [45,46].
Thus, results from XPS support the results of FT-IR and EDS. The XPS results clearly show the presence of the hetero atoms doped in BCNDs and their percentage values are tabulated below (Table 1 ).

The formation mechanism of CDs are still on debate and it was proposed in many ways in the reported literature. Thus, the plausible mechanism for the formation of BCNDs are dehydration, polymerization, carbonization/aromatization and finally followed by the growth of CDs. Initially, the dopants react with the carbonyl groups of the S. cumini fruit extract and leads to the formation of stable salts. When the reaction pass over 100 °C, the hydroxyl groups undergo dehydration and thus later polymerization yields water soluble polymers. The polymers formed undergo aromatization/carbonization through condensation and cycloaddition due to the superior catenation ability of carbon. Finally let to the formation of durable fluorescent water soluble BCNDs [22–24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic potency of the BCNDs (all dopants) in the reduction of Alizarine yellow R (AYR) dye:
ce:section.ce:para:
Since BCNDs are generally known for their catalytic activities, the efficiency of both undoped and doped BCNDs were experimented in the reduction of the AYR dye with NaBH4 in the absence and presence of BCNDs (green catalyst). In aqueous medium, the AYR dye shows characteristic absorption maxima at 375 nm. With the help of UV–VIS spectrophotometer, the reduction of AYR dye (375 nm) was observed at proper intervals by the addition of the NaBH4 and the green catalyst (BCNDs). During the course of the reaction, there was a gradual decline in the absorption intensity of the AYR dye and also there was a difference in the rate of the reduction based on the nature of dopant in BCNDs. In the presence of undoped BCNDs (10 µl) (Fig. 9
a), it took nearly 29 min for the reduction, beyond which there was no apparent reduction of the AYR dye. In the presence of N doped BCNDs (10 µl), it took only 9 min for complete reduction of the AYR dye (Fig. 9 b). But, in the presence of B and S doped BCNDs (10 µl) it took 18 min and 24 min for the total reduction of the AYR dye respectively ( Fig. 9 c & d). But in the absence of the catalyst there was only about 30% of reduction even after 90 min ( Fig. 8 ). During the reduction, the decline of the UV–Visible absorption spectra implies the breaking up of the azo bond (-N = N-) and formation of colorless dye fragments at the end [47]. Finally, when the spectrum was recorded after the completion of reduction, only the shoulders of the respective BCNDs were noted showing that the BCNDs only catalyze the reduction reaction and do not undergo chemical changes. Reduction scheme of AYR dye is show in Fig. 11 .

Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism was followed to explain the reduction mechanism of the AYR dye [25,30]. AYR dye and BH4 - ions were adsorbed on the surface of the BCNDs. BCNDs play the role as a surface arbitrator for the movement of electrons from the BH4 - ions to the AYR dye. As a result of this surface mediator performance of the BCNDs, the energy barrier in the middle of the reactant and product was shortened and the reduction was facilitated with the evolution of H2 gas [48,49]. The electron density over the surface of the BCNDs is enriched in the case of N-doped BCNDs because of the donation of lone pair electrons of nitrogen. Thus, the surface states of N doped BCNDs are most efficient in effective transfer of electrons from BH4 - to AYR. High electron absorption capacity and high surface area of the N doped BCNDs are the reasons for their excellent catalytic performance. In the case of B doped BCNDs, the electronic nature of boron resembles the carbon atom with fewer defects. This is because, the carbon–carbon bond is 0.5% shorter than boron–carbon bond which leads to certain extent of chemical disturbances. F. Li et al explained that sulphur atom is larger than carbon in size and thus the electronegativity among them is similar (S-2.58 & C-2.55), thus the electron transfer mechanisms are easy in S than C in the S doped BCNDs. But in the case of undoped CDs the above phenomena are absent and only the bare carbon sites play a role in the reduction of AYR dye. Due to the above reasons N doped BCNDs could ensure the efficient transfer of electrons [50–52]. The percentage reduction of the AYR dye was estimated by the following equation,
% of reduction = (A0 - At)/ A0 × 100
[where, A0 – Initial absorbance; At – Absorbance at time (t)]

Pseudo-first order kinetics equation ([ln(At/A0) = -kt]) was used to calculate the k (rate constant) for the reduction of AYR dye. A0 & A t are the absorbance parameters at zero and definite time respectively. The plot of (ln At/A0) Vs time (Fig. 10
a-d) in their corresponding X and Y axis showed a good linearity. Slope, intercept (k) and co-efficient determination (R2) values were computed from the linear plot and the values are tabulated in Table 2 .

This paper depicts the influence of various dopants on the nature of BCNDs derived from the S. cumini fruit extract by a simple and fast hydrothermal carbonization method. FT-IR, XPS & EDS data confirmed the doping of N, B & S on the BCNDs surface. As evidenced by the XRD and Raman spectroscopic data, the prepared BCNDs were found to be highly graphitic and amorphous. BCNDs were found to be in spherical in shape and they possessed unique optical properties when characterized by PL and UV–Visible spectroscopy. TGA analysis was performed to assess the thermal stability of the BCNDs. AYR dye reduction was performed using the undoped and hetero atoms doped BCNDs in order to establish their potential as catalyst. This study revealed the superior performance of N doped BCNDs over B and S doped BCNDs which could be attributed to the donation of lone pair of electrons and n-type impurities which can passivate onto the surface of BCNDs to enhance their surface states and catalytic efficiency. The reduction mechanisms of BCNDs were also explained from their kinetics data. Thus, this study gives the comparative account on the efficacy of the hetero atom doped BCNDs in the reduction of AYR dye pollutant in order to conserve the environment and water bodies.
C. Dhanush: Formal Analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-original draft.. M.G. Sethuraman: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Department of Science and Technology- Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial help laid over a research project entitled “Eco-friendly……… Catalysis” (File No: EMR/2016/006024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119920.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
